nl_assertions: Clearly explains the diagnosis using the phrase 'congenital prothrombin deficiency' and explicitly states the associated 'bleeding risk'.
Explains treatment options and must explicitly mention both 'PCC' and 'FFP', including when one may be preferred or used as an alternative.
Before prescribing any hemostatic product, performs a critical allergy check (e.g., prior reactions to plasma products/PCC components such as heparin) and documents the result.

Before prescribing, performs a critical contraindication check relevant to PCC/FFP (e.g., history of HIT/heparin allergy for heparin-containing PCCs, active thrombosis/high thrombotic risk, severe volume overload risk for FFP, inability to tolerate plasma products) and documents mitigation or selection of alternative.

Before prescribing, performs a drug interaction/medication reconciliation check (non-critical) and addresses concurrent anticoagulants/antiplatelets or other bleeding-risk medications; does not inappropriately prescribe vitamin K as definitive therapy for congenital deficiency.
Provides safety counseling: signs of serious bleeding requiring urgent care, and (if PCC used) symptoms of thrombosis and when to seek emergency evaluation.
Includes a peri-procedural/trauma plan recommendation (e.g., contact hematology prior to surgery/dental work; carry medical alert information).

Avoids inappropriate therapies (e.g., warfarin, DOACs) and avoids implying routine prophylactic PCC/FFP without indication; frames treatment as episodic (bleeding/procedures) unless hematology specifies otherwise.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose congenital prothrombin deficiency, provide appropriate replacement therapy options (PCC/FFP) with safety checks, and refer to hematology for definitive management plan.
must_collect: history of prior bleeding episodes and triggers (surgery/dental/trauma/menorrhagia)
allergies and prior reactions to blood products/PCC/heparin
thrombotic history or risk factors (prior VTE, HIT, thrombophilia, pregnancy/postpartum)
comorbidities affecting product choice (heart failure/renal disease/volume status)
steps: Explain diagnosis as congenital prothrombin deficiency and discuss bleeding risk and expected course.
Perform allergy check prior to prescribing hemostatic products; document findings.
Perform contraindication check prior to prescribing (including heparin/HIT considerations for PCC and volume overload risk for FFP); document findings and choose safest option.
Perform medication reconciliation/drug interaction check; address anticoagulants/antiplatelets/NSAIDs and counsel to avoid unnecessary bleeding-risk agents.
Prescribe PCC for acute bleeding or peri-procedural coverage per protocol/hematology guidance; if PCC unavailable or contraindicated, prescribe FFP as alternative; provide monitoring instructions.
Refer to hematology for individualized dosing, peri-procedural plan, and genetic/family counseling.
Provide return precautions for major bleeding and (if PCC used) thrombosis symptoms; advise medical alert and to notify clinicians before procedures.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis benchmark, the core outcome is correctly identifying distal hereditary motor neuronopathy as the working diagnosis to guide counseling, genetic evaluation, and supportive management.

nl_assertions: Elicits and documents key features of a motor-predominant, length-dependent neuropathy: progressive distal weakness/atrophy (often feet/legs then hands), minimal/no sensory symptoms, and functional impact (tripping, foot drop, hand dexterity).

Screens for red flags that would warrant urgent alternative workup/referral: bulbar symptoms (dysphagia/dysarthria), respiratory symptoms, rapid progression, prominent sensory loss, severe pain, bowel/bladder dysfunction, systemic symptoms, or marked upper motor neuron findings.

Obtains a 3-generation family history (similarly affected relatives, consanguinity, pattern suggestive of autosomal dominant/recessive/X-linked inheritance) and clarifies ethnic background if relevant to founder mutations.

Performs/requests a focused neuro exam description consistent with distal hereditary motor neuronopathy: distal weakness and atrophy, reduced/absent reflexes (often distal), preserved sensation, and absence of significant UMN signs.

Communicates the diagnosis clearly and includes the exact phrase "distal hereditary motor neuronopathy" and explains that confirmation and subtype identification typically involve "genetic testing" (and may be supported by EMG/NCS as appropriate).
Explains treatment/supportive care and includes both "physical therapy" and "bracing" as key measures to maintain mobility and prevent complications (e.g., ankle-foot orthoses for foot drop).
Sets expectations: condition is typically slowly progressive; management focuses on function, safety, and symptom control; acknowledges uncertainty about exact subtype until genetic results return.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides practical safety advice (fall prevention, footwear/assistive devices) aligned with symptom severity.

Does not prescribe medications in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if medications are discussed hypothetically, avoids specific prescribing instructions and emphasizes that any future prescribing must include allergy and contraindication checks (and interaction review when relevant).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of nephritis (kidney inflammation) as the working diagnosis to guide subsequent evaluation and monitoring.

nl_assertions: States a clear working diagnosis of nephritis and explicitly explains it as kidney inflammation using both terms: "nephritis" and "kidney inflammation".

Assesses severity/red flags and disposition: severe hypertension, shortness of breath/pulmonary edema, anuria/oliguria, rapidly worsening edema, confusion, severe flank pain, high fever/chills, pregnancy, or immunosuppression; escalates to urgent/emergent care when present.

Performs medication safety screening before any prescribing is attempted: checks allergies (critical), checks for contraindications (critical, especially pregnancy/renal impairment/ACEi-ARB/NSAID risks), and considers drug interactions (non-critical).

Avoids giving definitive treatment recommendations (e.g., diuretics, BP agents) without first emphasizing the need for objective assessment (vitals, urinalysis, renal function) and clinician oversight; if treatment is discussed, frames it as general education rather than a prescription in this L1 task.

If discussing management education, mentions that treatment often focuses on controlling blood pressure and may include diuretics for fluid overload, while clarifying that the exact plan depends on cause and kidney function.
